caveats: a: Occupation groups do not reveal task shares, so the exposure mapping is judgment rather than a measured AI quantity.
a: The code mixes data-heavy herd services, route services, and highly physical animal-care businesses.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers, which can be material in farrier, breeder, trainer, and shearing services.
a: The assigned compensation ratio has a 2024-wage versus 2022-receipts vintage mismatch and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
rho: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a.
rho: USDA evidence primarily measures technology on animal-production farms rather than implementation inside outsourced support firms.
rho: Animal welfare and liability considerations can require human verification even when analytics are reliable.
e: The dataset supplies 34 firms using an estimated margin bridge rather than observed firm EBITDA.
e: The farming and agriculture margin proxy used for n may misclassify service firms because margins differ across boarding, breeding, records, poultry, and mobile field services.
e: OEWS includes employers of all sizes and does not identify which belong in the target earnings band.
e: Some horse boarding economics may be driven more by real estate than a separable service operation.
s5: The proxy is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed transfers.
s5: Owner dependence varies sharply among records platforms, route services, skilled practitioners, and facility-based boarding.
s5: An asset sale or family handoff without a transferable operating company is not a qualifying event.
q: No source directly measures the retained share of implemented AI benefits.
q: Value capture differs materially between subscription-like record services, fixed-price routes, skilled practitioner work, and cost-sensitive farm labor services.
q: Precision technology can benefit the customer directly, increasing pressure to share savings.
d5: The BLS output projection combines crop, animal, and forestry support activities.
d5: No public series provides constant-quality demand for the heterogeneous services inside NAICS 115210.
d5: Dairy technology evidence does not represent poultry, equine, sheep, or companion-animal service demand.
o: The service mix ranges from almost entirely physical work to data and record services that are much more self-serviceable.
o: Technology adoption by farms can complement outsourced providers or displace them, and available evidence does not resolve the net effect.
o: A retained operator may use a materially different staffing model.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are livestock farms, dairies, poultry operations, horse owners and farms, breeders, breed associations, and related animal operators. Revenue recurs through per-animal, per-visit, per-day, per-head, hourly, route, subscription, testing, record, or seasonal service charges; some contracts bundle labor, equipment, consumables, facilities, data, and scheduled availability.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal farm units; animal raising for sale; veterinary practices; pet boarding and grooming classified outside this industry; racehorse training; shells; passive real-estate or financing vehicles; and firms outside the specified earnings band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced animal-production support to external livestock, equine, and companion-animal customers, including breeding and semen services, pedigree records, dairy herd improvement, horse boarding and training outside racing, farrier work, poultry catching and house cleaning, livestock spraying, sheep dipping and shearing, and related classified support work.

a.bridge: BLS directly measures employment and wages by occupation for NAICS 115200, the published industry corresponding to support activities for animal production, but it does not measure task-level AI exposure. I map most office, business, computer, record, scheduling, customer-service, and selected management work, plus portions of animal monitoring, breeding analysis, inspection, training documentation, and route planning, to current not-yet-automated exposure. This can overstate exposure where physical delivery dominates and understate computer-vision opportunity embedded in animal-care roles.
caveats: Occupation groups do not reveal task shares, so the exposure mapping is judgment rather than a measured AI quantity.
The code mixes data-heavy herd services, route services, and highly physical animal-care businesses.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers, which can be material in farrier, breeder, trainer, and shearing services.
The assigned compensation ratio has a 2024-wage versus 2022-receipts vintage mismatch and a cross-code harmonization adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task survey across breeding, dairy-record, equine, poultry, shearing, farrier, and boarding firms that distinguishes already automated software from remaining work would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is dominated by hands-on work: farming occupations are 40.09 percent of employment and personal care and service occupations are 26.75 percent, while office support is 7.99 percent and management is 6.34 percent. Higher wages in management, business, science, sales, computer, and office roles raise their wage-weighted importance. AI can automate records, scheduling, billing, customer communication, pedigree and herd-data analysis, monitoring triage, and some planning, but cannot directly replace most animal handling, farrier, shearing, cleaning, boarding, and equipment tasks.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader support activities for agriculture and forestry at 1.6 percent annually from 2024 to 2034, equivalent to roughly 1.08 over five years. I center just below that figure because the source includes crop and forestry support, while animal-support demand depends on livestock populations, farm consolidation, equine spending, and customer self-service. The broader proxy could overstate or understate this industry depending on those mix effects.
caveats: The BLS output projection combines crop, animal, and forestry support activities.
No public series provides constant-quality demand for the heterogeneous services inside NAICS 115210.
Dairy technology evidence does not represent poultry, equine, sheep, or companion-animal service demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price revenue and service-volume data for NAICS 115210, segmented by breeding, records, dairy improvement, equine, poultry, shearing, farrier, and other support activities, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Animal production continues to require breeding, care, handling, records, improvement, cleaning, and seasonal specialist work, while farm consolidation can favor scaled outside providers. Precision livestock technology may create demand for data and monitoring support but can also automate or internalize traditional tasks. The service mix supports modest real growth with downside from livestock cycles and self-service.

caveats: The dataset supplies 34 firms using an estimated margin bridge rather than observed firm EBITDA.
The farming and agriculture margin proxy used for n may misclassify service firms because margins differ across boarding, breeding, records, poultry, and mobile field services.
OEWS includes employers of all sizes and does not identify which belong in the target earnings band.
Some horse boarding economics may be driven more by real estate than a separable service operation.
e.change_evidence: A diligence sample of the dataset-supplied LMM candidates showing primary activity, normalized EBITDA, owner dependence, recurring revenue, customer concentration, facility ownership, management depth, and control transferability would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Most classified businesses sell recurring or repeat support services to external customers, so the default lens fits. Eligibility is reduced by owner-dependent practices, businesses inseparable from a specific trainer or breeder, captive farm units, real-estate-heavy horse boarding, weak management depth, customer concentration, misclassification, and services whose licenses, reputation, or relationships do not transfer. Multi-employee route, testing, record, breeding, poultry, and boarding operators are more likely to qualify.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 115210 ($1,510,014,410) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 115210 ($4,944,820,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $214,749 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 115210); class <5: 3,779 firms, avg revenue/firm $360,463 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $28,981 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 640 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,161,781 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $93,407 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,285,610 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $183,763 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,008,422 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $402,677 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,983,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,571,494 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,319,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,894,853 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 34

caveats: The service mix ranges from almost entirely physical work to data and record services that are much more self-serviceable.
Technology adoption by farms can complement outsourced providers or displace them, and available evidence does not resolve the net effect.
A retained operator may use a materially different staffing model.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level make-versus-buy studies and adoption data showing which animal-support tasks move to farm software, robotics, internal staff, or equipment vendors would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Physical animal handling, breeding procedures, hoof care, shearing, poultry catching and cleaning, boarding, training, and on-site response still require an accountable service operator. Pedigree records, herd improvement analysis, scheduling, monitoring, and some customer support can migrate to producer-operated software, sensors, robotics, or vertically integrated farm staff, so not all year-five quantity necessarily remains with an outsourced operator of this kind.

caveats: No source directly measures the retained share of implemented AI benefits.
Value capture differs materially between subscription-like record services, fixed-price routes, skilled practitioner work, and cost-sensitive farm labor services.
Precision technology can benefit the customer directly, increasing pressure to share savings.
q.change_evidence: Representative service contracts, price histories, renewal outcomes, and branch-level margin panels before and after automation, segmented by service model, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-animal, per-visit, route, boarding, and fixed service pricing can let operators retain gains until contracts or price lists reset. Local availability, trust, animal-handling quality, records, and specialized skill provide differentiation. Retention is reduced by farm-customer price sensitivity, bidding and renewal negotiation, transparent hourly or per-head rates, customer consolidation, and software or equipment vendors claiming part of the benefit.

caveats: The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a.
USDA evidence primarily measures technology on animal-production farms rather than implementation inside outsourced support firms.
Animal welfare and liability considerations can require human verification even when analytics are reliable.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor and operator cohorts reporting five-year adoption, task-hours avoided, error rates, customer acceptance, and realized savings for animal-service scheduling, records, vision, and herd analytics would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Scheduling, billing, customer communications, record processing, workforce coordination, and document workflows can be implemented quickly. Herd and breeding analytics, computer vision, sensor triage, and field decision support are feasible but depend on clean identifiers, customer data access, connectivity, animal-specific validation, workflow redesign, and human review for welfare, safety, and service quality. Small fragmented operators may lack implementation capacity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries rather than completed control transfers in animal-production support. I discount intentions for noncompletion, intrafamily transfers, owner-dependent reputation and skill, retained equine real estate, and internal reorganizations, while allowing that fragmented agricultural service ownership can generate succession supply. The proxy may overstate saleability of practitioner businesses or understate consolidation transactions.
caveats: The proxy is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed transfers.
Owner dependence varies sharply among records platforms, route services, skilled practitioners, and facility-based boarding.
An asset sale or family handoff without a transferable operating company is not a qualifying event.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year closed-transaction data for NAICS 115210 from GF Data, Axial, PitchBook, agricultural lenders, equine advisers, and animal-service brokers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan interviews, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful sale or transfer intent, and recurring local animal-support firms can attract strategic or individual buyers. Completed qualifying transfers are less frequent because specialized expertise, customer relationships, animal-handling reputation, real estate, and dependence on the incumbent can prevent a planned succession from becoming a transferable control transaction.

principal_driver: A recurring outsourced-service model, measurable office and management labor, and expanding livestock data create implementable AI opportunities alongside durable physical service demand.
principal_weakness: The small firm pool is margin-estimated, and business-model heterogeneity makes both transferability and technology-driven self-service highly candidate-specific.
